translations.source_text: 興滅國、繼絕世、擧逸民、天下之民歸心焉。
translations.translation:
Zhou Yu (Gongjin)
周瑜 (公瑾)
Lived AD 175–210
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 54 (Wu 9)
byZL181
onMarch 18th, 2025

Zhou Yu, styledGongjin, was fromShu[舒],Lujiang[廬江]. The cousin of his grandfather was[Zhou] Jing, and the son of[Zhou] Jingwas[Zhou] Zhong. They both served asGrand CommandantofHan.AHis father was [Zhou] Yi [周異],PrefectofLuoyang[洛陽令].
TheBook of Later HanbyXie Chengstates:[Zhou] Jingwas styledZhongxiang. He was praised for his incorruptibility and capability when he was young, and due to his erudite knowledge, he was nominated asFilial and Incorruptand recruited by the bureau of theExcellencies. Later, he served asInspectorofYu province[豫州刺史]. He recruitedChen FanofRunan[汝南] to serve asAssistant Official, and recruitedLi Ying,Xun Gun, and Du Mi [杜密] ofYingchuan[潁川], and Zhu Yu [朱㝢] ofPei state[沛國] to serve asClerk. They would all be heroic and outstanding gentlemen of the realm. Later, he was promoted toDirector of the Secretariat, then ascended to becomeGrand Commandant.
TheAnnals of HanbyZhang Fanstates: The father of[Zhou] Jing, [Zhou] Rong [周榮], served asDirector of the Secretariatduring the reigns of theGentleandModerateEmperors. When[Zhou] Jingfirst served as a governor, he was fond of being virtuous and adored scholars. Each year he recommended theFilial and Incorrupt, he would invite them in, and at the rear hall, they would feast together with his family. This was the case four times. The gifts for their departure were always prepared, and he would also nominate their younger relatives. He often said, “These ministers are as my children; what difficulty is there in placing them in governance?”1Before this, theExcellency over the Masses,Han Yan, served asGrand AdministratorofHenei[河內太守]. He had no private [dealings] in public [business], and to those he recommended, he would just say one farewell and nothing more. Later, he would also not involve their relatives,2saying, “My nominations are already appropriate as they are. I will not extend any grace and favoritism to any family.” At the time, some debaters satirized the two ofthem.•••
Some versions of the text write the last part of the quote as “what difficulty is there in placing them in such?” [於之何有？], with such referring to the government in the context of the nominations.↩
Han Yanwould not nominate their relatives, in contrast toZhou Jing.↩
[Zhou] Yuwas tall and strong, and he was handsome. Previously, whenSun Jianraised righteous troops to destroyDong Zhuo, he moved his family toShu[舒]. The son of[Sun] Jian,[Sun] Ce, was of the same age as[Zhou] Yu, and they were especially friendly.[Zhou] Yugave up a great mansion on the south part of a road to accommodate[Sun] Ce, then entered the hall to pay respects to hismother. They would share what they had for what they did not have.
The cousin of the father of[Zhou] Yu,[Zhou] Shang, served asGrand AdministratorofDanyang[丹楊太守].[Zhou] Yuwent to visit him. At the time,[Sun] Cewas going to ferry east,3and when he arrived atLiyang[歷陽], he sent a letter to[Zhou] Yuby post horses.[Zhou] Yuled troops to join[Sun] Ce.[Sun] Cewas greatly delighted and said, “Having you, sir, is fulfilling!” He thus followed to attackHengjiang[橫江] andDangli[當利], all of which were conquered. And they ferried across, then attackedMoling[秣陵], defeatingZe RongandXue Li, and then turned and tookHushu[湖孰],Jiangcheng[江乘], and enteredQu’e[曲阿], which causedLiu Yaoto flee. The army of[Sun] Cewas already in the tens of thousands. He thus told[Zhou] Yu, “This army is enough for me to seize theWu-Kuairegion [吳會], and to pacify the Shanyue. You, sir, should return to guardDanyang[丹楊].” And so[Zhou] Yureturned.•••
That is to say, ferrying east across the Yangzi river.↩
Shortly after,Yuan Shusent his younger cousin,[Yuan] Yin, to replace[Zhou] ShangasGrand Administrator, so[Zhou] Yuand[Zhou] Shangboth returned toShouchun.[Yuan] Shuwanted to have[Zhou] Yube his general, but[Zhou] Yuobserved that[Yuan] Shuhave no success in the end, and hence asked to beChiefofJuchao, wanting to borrow the roads there to return east.[Yuan] Shuheeded him. He then returned toWufromJuchao. That year was the third year of theera [198].[Sun] Cepersonally welcomed[Zhou] Yu, made himHousehold General who Establishes Might, and gave him two thousand troops and fifty cavalry.B[Zhou] Yuwas twenty-four at the time, so all ofWucalled him “Zhou the Youth”. Because the grace and sincerity of[Zhou] Yuwas known inLujiang, he went toNiuzhuto defend it, and later becameChiefofChungu.
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates:[Sun] Cealso gave[Zhou] Yudrums and trumpets, and a residence was made for him in the government seat; there were no gifts that could compare to his.[Sun] Ceordered, “ZhouGongjinis a heroic personage and an extraordinary talent. He and I have a friendship of bound hair4and a relationship of flesh and blood. When he was inDanyangin the past, he sent troops and boats with grain to aid in the great undertaking. In evaluating his kindness and requiting his achievements, these are not even enough to repayhim.”•••
Here, “friendship of bound hair” [總角之好] is a metaphor for close childhood friends, as the hair of children were typically bond in knots.↩
Shortly after,[Sun] Cedesired to takeJing province, and appointed[Zhou] YuasCentral Army ProtectorandGrand AdministratorofJiangxia. He followed in the attack ofWan, capturing it. At the time, they obtained the two daughters of Lord Qiao, both national beauties.[Sun] Cetook in theelder Qiaowhile[Zhou] Yutook in thejunior Qiao.CHe again advanced toXunyang, defeatingLiu Xun, and invadedJiangxia. He returned to settleYuzhang,Luling, and remained to guardBaqiu.D
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates:[Sun] Cejested to[Zhou] Yuwith a carefree expression, “Although the two daughters of Lord Qiao are stunning, they have gotten us two as their husbands, which is also sufficient cause for joy!”
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would investigate this. WhenSun Cefirst tookYuzhangandLuling, he could not have yet settledJiangxia. Where[Zhou] Yuwas defending corresponds with present-day Baqiu county; it and theBaqiuwhere he later died in are not the same locations.
In the fifth year [200],[Sun] Cedied, and[Sun] Quantook over responsibilities.[Zhou] Yuled troops to attend the funeral, then remained inWu. Because he wasCentral Army Protector, he andChief ClerkZhang Zhaomanaged domestic affairsjointly.E
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates: Shortly afterExcellency CaodefeatedYuan Shao, the might of his army grew stronger by the day, and in the seventh year of theera [202], he sent a message to demand[Sun] Quanto send a child as a hostage with the pretense of appointing them as an official.[Sun] Quanassembled the ministers to discuss.Zhang ZhaoandQin Song, among others, were hesitant and indecisive.[Sun] Quandid not want to send a hostage, and he led only[Zhou] Yuto go before his mother to make a decision.[Zhou] Yusaid, “In the times of yore, the state of Chu was first enfeoffed along the sides ofJing mountain, their territory not even one hundredli. Yet, their successors were virtuous and capable. The land expanded and frontiers opened up, then they established their foundation at Ying, and then they tookJing provinceandYang province, even reachingNanhai. Such a hereditary undertaking with continuous fortune lasted for over nine hundred years. Now you,General, have inherited the surplus resources of yourfatherand yourelder brother. You have troops across six commanderies, your soldiers are proficient and your provisions are plenty, and your generals and officers would undertake your command. You can cast from the mountains to make copper and you can boil the seawater to make salt. Within your lands, there is a great abundance, and the people do not think about causing turmoil. Boats with raised sails that travel on water can leave in the morning and arrive in the night. Our soldiers are tenacious and courageous, and wherever we go, we have no foe. What is there to be pressured by, such that you want to send a hostage? Once the hostage enters [the land ofCao Cao], we have no choice but to conjoin ourselves with the Cao clan from head to tail, and then being conjoined to them from head to tail, we have no choice but to go upon receiving summons, and then we would be curbed by other people. At most, we would not even get something beyond a seal of a marquis, over ten servants, and a couple of chariots and horses. How can that be the same as facing the south and proclaiming yourself independent?5It would be better not to send [a hostage], but to observe them for any changes. If the Cao clan is capable of rectifying the world through righteousness, it would not be too late for you,General, to serve them, but if they plan to wreck havoc, messing around with troops is like fire: if uninhibited, they will burn themselves!General, you can resist their might through your military strategies and courage, and thus await theMandate of Heaven; what need is there to send a hostage?” Themotherof[Sun] Quansaid, “WhatGongjindiscussed is right.Gongjinwas of the same age asBofu, only a month younger. I see him as a son, and you should treat him like an elder brother.” And so, no hostage wassent.•••
Here, “facing the south and proclaiming yourself independent” [南面稱孤] is the same as declaring himself an independent ruler, and the throne tended to face the south.↩
In the eleventh year [206], he supervisedSun Yu, among others, invaded two garrisons at Ma and Bao. They hung the heads of their leaders and captured over ten thousand people alive. They returned to guardGuanting lake. TheGrand AdministratorofJiangxia,Huang Zu, sent his general,Deng Long, to lead one thousand troops to enterChaisang, but[Zhou] Yupursued and attacked them. He captured[Deng] Longalive and sent him toWu.
In the thirteenth year [208], spring,[Sun] QuaninvadedJiangxia.[Zhou] Yuserved Grand Commander of the Front. In the ninth month of that year, whenExcellency CaoenteredJing province,Liu Congsurrendered, andExcellency Caoobtained his navy. His ships and infantry numbered in the hundreds of thousands. When the generals and officers heard about this, they were all terrified.[Sun] Quaninvited his subordinates and asked them for a strategy. Those discussing said, "Excellency Caois like a dhole and a tiger. He uses his title of the Chancellor ofHanas his pretext and coerces theSon of Heavenso that he can conquer the four directions, and in whatever action he takes, he cites the imperial court as his excuse. Today, if we oppose him, things will not go favorably, and what is more, according to the overall situation,General, the only entity with which we can oppose[Cao] Caois theYangzi River. However,[Cao] Caohas now obtainedJing provinceand annexed its lands, and whenLiu Biaowas training his navy, he had Mengchong ships6and Doujian ships7in the thousands, and now all of these have been placed along theriverby[Cao] Cao. These will go downstream through naval and land routes along with infantry troops. In this regard, the natural defense of theYangzi Riveris shared by him alongside us. The strong and the weak in terms of power and strength also does not need to be debated. We believe that for our overall plan, it would be better to receive him.” But[Zhou] Yusaid:•••
These were offensive ships covered with leather that could go at a high speed with its many oars. People could shoot out of the windows of the ship.↩
These were very agile ships, with oars allowing it great mobility. It also had parapets built into them.↩
“This is not so. Even though[Cao] Caouses his title of the Chancellor ofHanas his pretext, he is the traitor of theHanin truth.General, you are divine in strength and mighty in talent, and you are also supported by the achievements of yourfatherand yourelder brother. We control theJiangdong regionand have thousands ofliin territory. Our soldiers are elite, our resources are sufficient, and our mighty heroes are glad with their duties. You should sweep across the world and eliminate filth and expel nastiness for theHan dynasty, and what is more,[Cao] Caois sending himself to his death, so can we receive him? I would ask to calculate [the situation] for you,General. Were it that the northern lands were already secure,[Cao] Caowould not have anything to worry domestically. If he is capable of fighting in the battlefield against us over a prolonged period of time, and if he is also capable of determining victory and defeat with us through warships, it may be possible [for him to win]. But right now, the northern lands have not yet been secured.Ma ChaoandHan Suiare still in theGuanxi region, and they will become future problems for[Cao] Cao. Moreover, he has abandoned saddles and cavalry, and now depends on boats and ships. Competing with us with those is not what the central lands excel at. Now that it is frigid, the horses have no fodder and grass. He has also urged the soldiers of the central lands to travel afar towards rivers and lakes; they are not trained to be naval warriors, and they will certainly fall ill. These four things are taboos8in warfare, and yet[Cao] Caois doing all of them in his rashness.General, your capture of[Cao] Caoshould be today! I beseech to have thirty thousand elite soldiers and to advance towardsXiakou! I will ensure that I will defeat him for you,General!”•••
The four things are presumablyMa ChaoandHan Suinot being taken care of, being ineffective at naval warfare, a lack of food for horses, and vulnerability to illness.↩
[Sun] Quansaid, “Thatold traitorhas wanted to depose theHanto establish himself for so long. He was only worried about the two Yuans,Lü Bu,Liu Biao, and me! Now, those lords have already been destroyed, and I am the only one who has survived. I will not coexist with thatold traitor! Sir, when you said we should attack, that was very much my selfsame [intention]. Heaven must have bestowed you tome!”F
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates:[Sun] Quanunsheathed his sword and sliced the desk for memorials before him. He said, “Any generals or officials who dare speak of welcoming[Cao] Caoshall befall the same fate as this desk!” And when it was nighttime of the day the assembly disbanded,[Zhou] Yurequested a meeting. He said, “The people have just seen the letter by[Cao] Cao, which purports a force of eight-hundred thousand naval and land troops. They were thus all afraid and are unable to calculate the actual situation, so they found it convenient to utter such suggestions with utterly no logic to them. Now I will determine the truth of this. The people of the central states that the enemy leads do not exceed one-hundred fifty to one-hundred sixty thousand in number, and moreover, that army has been ill for a long time. The troops of[Liu] Biaothat they obtained also number seventy to eighty thousand at the maximum, but they harbor misgivings. When ill troops command hesitant soldiers, even though the number of the army may be great, they would be too insignificant to be feared. If I get fifty thousand elite troops, that would be enough for me to resist them. I hope you will have no doubts,General.”[Sun] Quanstroked his back and said, “Gongjin, all of your words thus far have been in concord with my thoughts. People likeZibuandWenbiaowere worried about their wives and children and stuck to their selfish concerns. They have let me down deeply. Only you andZijingare of the selfsame [mind] as me. Heaven must have had you two assist me. Fifty thousand troops are difficult to assemble in a hurry, but I have already selected thirty thousand men. The ships, provisions, and battle equipment are all ready. You,Zijing, andLord Chengwill set out and be at the front, while I will continue to send out troops with many supplies and provisions to serve as your rear support, sir. If you can handle them, sir, then engage in a decisive battle. Should developments not be as you expected, then you should retreat to me when you can. I will then engage in a decisive battle withMengde.”
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, has a thought. The proposal to resistExcellency Caoactually began withLu Su. At the time,Zhou Yuwas sent toPoyang, but[Lu] Suurged[Sun] Quanto summon[Zhou] Yu, so when[Zhou] Yureturned fromPoyang, he and[Lu] Sudid not know they were in accord. Thus, they were able to achieve great accomplishments together. The base biography states plainly, "[Sun] Quan invited his subordinates and asked them for a strategy.”[Zhou] Yudisregarded the opinion of the masses and was the only one who talked of a plan of resistance. Perhaps why there was no mention of[Lu] Subeing the first with such a plan was probably because it would rob the good deed of[Lu] Su.
At the time,Liu Beiwas defeated byExcellency Cao. [Liu Bei] wanted to retreat south, so he ferried across theYangzi Riverand encounteredLu Sumet atDangyang. Together, they made a plan, then advanced toXiakou.Zhuge Liangwas sent to[Sun] Quan, and[Sun] Quansent[Zhou] YuandCheng Pu, among others, to join forces with[Liu] Beito resistExcellency Cao, encountering them atChibi. At the time, the soldiers ofExcellency Caowere sick, and when they first fought, the army of theExcellencywas defeated. They retreated and resided north of theYangzi River, while[Zhou] Yuand the others were at the south bank. The subordinate general of[Zhou] Yu,Huang Gai, said, “Right now, the enemy is numerous while we are few. It will be difficult for us to hold on over time. However, I see that the warships of[Cao] Cao, from beginning to end, are linked together. We can burn them to make them retreat.” Thus, they took tens of Mengchong ships and Doujian ships, filled them with firewood, poured oil into them, wrapped them with large curtains, and erected ivory-tipped banners. They wrote a letter beforehand to report toExcellency Cao, feigning surrender.GThey also prepared speedy boats that were each tied to the back of the large warships, and thus went forward together in their order. The military officers ofExcellency Caoall craned their necks and watched afar, and they said that this was[Huang] Gaisurrendering.[Huang] Gaithen unleashed the warships and set off a fire at the same time. At the time, the wind was very fierce, so it spread the fire onto the camps on the banks. Soon after, the smoke and flames reached the heavens. The number of men and horses that incinerated or drowned were too many. The army thus was defeated and they withdrew back to protectNan commandery.H[Liu] Beiand[Zhou] Yu, among others, pursued them.Excellency CaohadCao Renand some others remain to guardJianglingcity, while he directly returned north.[Zhou] YuandCheng Puadvanced toNan commanderyand faced off with[Cao] Ren, with each of them separated by the greatYangzi River. The armies had not yet clashedIwhen[Zhou] YusentGan Ningahead to seizeYiling.[Cao] Rensent some infantry and cavalry to attack and siege[Gan] Ning.[Gan] Ningreported this as an emergency to[Zhou] Yu.[Zhou] Yuthus used the plan ofLü Meng; he hadLing Tongremain to guard the rear while he and[Lü] Mengwent to rescue[Gan] Ning. The siege of[Gan] Ningwas thus lifted, then ferried across to camp at the north bank and set a date to engage in a large battle.[Zhou] Yupersonally mounted a horse to break the formation, but he was hit by a flying arrow at the right side of his body, and it was an extreme wound. Thus, he withdrew. Later,[Cao] Renheard that[Zhou] Yuhad not gotten up from rest, so he commanded troops to the formation. However,[Zhou] Yuhad himself get up, patrolled the military barracks, and encouraged the officers.[Cao] Renthus retreated.
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Rivercontains the letter of[Huang] Gai, which states:
“I,[Huang] Gai, once received favor and grace from the Sun clan. I often served as their general, and I was treated rather well. However, seeing the overall situation of world affairs, to use the people of the six commanderies of theJiangdong regionand the Shanyue to match the one million troops of the central state is a situation where the strong and the weak are not on par; this can be seen by all of thosewithin the Seas. The generals and officials of theeast, no matter their stupidity or intelligence, all know it is impossible [to resist]. OnlyZhou YuandLu Suremain obstinate in their shallowness and stupidity, and they have not yet come to the realization. To submit to the mandate today is a true plan. Whatever[Zhou] Yuis in command over can be easily defeated. On the day of the battle, I will be at the front. I will take advantage of the situation to defect and devote myself to your command nearby.”
Excellency Caoarranged a special meeting with the messenger and discreetly questioned him. He then verbally instructed him, “My only fear is that this is a ruse by you. If what[Huang] Gaisaid is sincere and true, then I shall bestow him peerage and rewards that will be beyond those he received throughout [fromWu].
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates: When the day of the battle came,[Huang] Gaiselected ten light and quick warships and loaded them with dry reeds and withered firewood, piling them inside. He inundated them with fish oil and covered them with red curtains. He set up gonfalons and dragon flags on top of the warships. At the time, the southeast wind was strong, and so the ten warships advanced rapidly. When they were in the middle of theYangzi River, they raised their sails.[Huang] Gailit a fire to inform the other officers to have the soldiers shout loudly in unison: “We are surrendering!” The military men of[Cao] Caoall left their camps to stand and watch. When they were over twoli9from the northern army, they set off a fire at the same time. The fire was fierce and the wind was fierce, and they went towards the boats like arrows, the flying dust utterly blazing. They burnt all the northern boats and spread it to the camps at the banks.[Zhou] Yuand the others led light and proficient [troops] to pursue their rear, beating thunder drums for a large advance. The northern army suffered a great loss andExcellency Caoretreated.•••
Assuming that oneliwas about 416 meters, twoliwould have been 0.83 kilometers, or 0.51 miles.↩
TheRecords of Wustates:[Liu] Beitold[Zhou] Yu, “[Cao] Renis guardingJianglingcity. The city has multiple provisions within it, enough to pose a serious threat. I will sendZhangYideto lead one thousand troops to follow you, but you will divide two thousand men of your own to accompany me so that I can follow along theXia riverand cut off the rear of[Cao] Ren. When[Cao] Renhears of my approach, he will surely flee.”[Zhou] Yuthus had two thousand troops aid him.
[Sun] Quanconferred[Zhou] YuasDeputy Generaland appointed him asGrand AdministratorofNan commandery.Xiajuan,Hanchang,Liuyang, andZhoulingwere made taxpaying cities for him. He garrisoned and occupiedJiangling.Liu Bei, asGeneral of the LeftandGovernorofJing province, had his government seat atGong’an. When[Liu] Beiwent to Jing[kou] to meet[Sun] Quan,[Zhou] Yupetitioned:
“Liu Beiis ambitious and powerful in demeanor, and he hasGuan YuandZhang Fei, generals who are as bears and tigers. They are certainly people who will not bend to others over a long time. In my humble opinion, the overall plan should be to move[Liu] Beiinto confinement inWu, then construct a palace that will have many beautiful girls and toys such that they will entertain his ears and eyes. As for the other two men, you should set up areas for the each of them, then order someone like me to lead them into offensive battles. The great undertaking can thus be settled. Right now, you are giving too much land in support. Those three men are gathered together, all in the battlefield. I fear once those scaled dragons get clouds and rain, they will never be creatures that remain in ponds!”
[Sun] Quan, becauseExcellency Caowas still in the north, wanted to extensively recruit the heroic and mighty, and he also feared that it would be difficult to curb[Liu] Beiin a hurry, so he did not accept this.
At the time,Liu ZhangwasGovernorofYi province. Outside of him, there wasZhang Lu, who was encroaching on him.[Zhou] Yuthus went to Jing[kou] to meet[Sun] Quan, and he said, “Now thatCao Caohas recently been beaten, his current worry is in his internal regions, so he is not yet able to engage in mutual clashes with you,General. I beseech to advance together with theGeneral who Exerts Authorityto take Shu, and once we obtain Shu, we will also annexZhang Lu. I would then have theGeneral who Exerts Authorityremain behind to firmly defend the land and establish good relations withMa Chao. I would then return to occupyXiangyangwith you,General, and then we can encroach on[Cao] Cao. We can thus plot for the north.”[Sun] Quanagreed to this.[Zhou] Yuthen returned toJianglingto prepare the luggage, but while on the roads at Baqiu, he died of illness.JHe was thirty-six at the time.[Sun] Quanwore white [mourning] clothes and wailed in mourning. His sentiment moved his attendants. The remains were to return back toWu, but he welcomed them atWuhu, and regarding the expenses for various things,10he provided for all of them. He later ordered, “If the former GeneralsZhou YuandCheng Puhad any tenants, there is to be no inquiry for the both of them.11”•••
It is heavily implied that the “various things” [眾事] were funerary things.↩
IfZhou YuandCheng Pusomehow got tenants without being given them by the government, there would be no investigation. Here, “tenants” [人客] probably refers to “tenant farmers” [佃客], who worked on the land for their landlord.↩
Your minister,[Pei] Songzhi, would investigate this. When[Zhou] Yuwanted to take Shu, he returned toJianglingto pack up. The place where he died corresponds with present-day Baling. It and theBaqiuwhere he previously garrisoned have the same name, but different locations.
***
Previously,[Zhou] Yuwas seen as a friend by[Sun] Ce, and theConsort Dowageralso instructed[Sun] Quanto treat him like an elder brother. At the time,[Sun] Quanwas just a General in rank, so the various generals and guests were casual in respecting him. It was[Zhou] Yuwho was the first to pay his full respects, and he adhered to the rites of a minister. He was magnanimous and lenient by nature, such that he was generally able to win over men. Only he andCheng Puwere not in harmony.K[Zhou] Yuhad been proficient at music since his youth, and even after three cups [of wine], if there was a mistake,[Zhou] Yuwould always be aware of it, and when he was aware of it, he would always turn. Thus, contemporaries made a ballad, which ran, “When the music errs,Zhou the Youthturns.”
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates:[Cheng] Pu, because he was older, had humiliated and insulted[Zhou] Yuon numerous occasions, but[Zhou] Yuwould be deigning and tolerating, and he never quarreled.[Cheng] Pulater respected him and valued him intimately, and he told people, “Interacting withZhouGongjinis like drinking mellow wine: you do not realize you are drunk!” Contemporaries believed his humility and deference won over people like in this case.
Previously, whenExcellency Caoheard that[Zhou] Yuwas young in age but had outstanding talent, he thought he could be persuaded over, so he secretly went toYang provinceand sentJiang GanofJiujiang commanderyto meet[Zhou] Yu.[Jiang] Ganhad an appearance, and he was praised for his argumentative talent. He walked alone12in theJianghuai region; no one could reply to him. With plain clothes and a hemp headscarf, he went to[Zhou] Yuunder the pretense of a private venture.[Zhou] Yuwent to welcome him, and he immediately told[Jiang] Gan, “Ziyi, truly have you toiled by wading afar across the rivers and lakes, and just to be a spokesperson for the Cao clan?”[Jiang] Gansaid, “We are of the same village, and we were isolated for some time. I heard about your beautiful achievements from afar, so I came to express my longing and to also see your elegant demeanor. Yet, you have said I am just a spokesperson. Are you not anticipating deceit from me?”[Zhou] Yusaid, “Although I am no peer of Kui and [Shi] Kuang,13hearing the strings and taking in the tune is enough for me to determine between harmony and dissonance [in the music].” He then invited[Jiang] Ganin, and he set up wine and a meal. When they were done, he sent him off, saying, “It happens that I have some crucial business now, so do depart to your lodging. Once the business is over, then I will invite you at another time.” After three days,[Zhou] Yuinvited[Jiang] Ganto tour the camp. While walking, they saw that the depositories were full of military supplies and weapons. They then returned to feast and drink, and he displayed his attendants, adorned dresses, and rare curios. He then told[Jiang] Gan, “When a great man interacts with the world, if he encounters a lord who understands him, then they should have the duty of lord and minister on the outside and also should have the close relationship of bone and flesh in the inside. His words are undertaken and his advice is followed, and they share disaster and fortune. Even ifSu [Qin]andZhang [Yi]were reborn, andElder Liremerged, he should still pat their backs but refuse their rhetoric. How can you who is younger be capable of swaying?”[Jiang] Ganonly laughed and said nothing else. When[Jiang] Ganreturned, he praised[Zhou] Yuof being magnanimous and noble, and that he could not become discontent by mere words. Many gentlemen of the central lands also praised him thus.
WhenLiu Beiwas to return from Jing[kou],[Sun] Quanrode on a large ship called “Flying Clouds”, and over ten people, including him,Zhang Zhao,Qin Song, andLu Su, among others, together sent him off and held a large banquet to bid their farewells.[Zhang] Zhaoand[Lu] Su, among others, left first, so only[Sun] Quanremained to talk with[Liu] Bei. Between words, he praised[Zhou] Yu, saying, “Gongjinhas civil and military talent, as well as having calculating strategy. He is an outstanding person among ten thousand people. Observing that his talent and capability are expansive and great, however, I fear that he will not be subordinate to anyone for a long time.” When[Zhou] Yudefeated theWeiarmy,Excellency Caosaid, “I am not ashamed to flee.” He later wrote to[Sun] Quan, stating, “At the battle ofChibi, there happened to be an illness, and my burning ships came back on their own. This unreasonably causedZhou Yuto falsely achieve such a reputation.” The might and fame of[Zhou] Yuspread far and wide, henceExcellency CaoandLiu Beiboth wanted to defame and put suspicion on him. When he died,[Sun] Quancried and wept, and he said, “Gongjinhad the ability to be an aide to kings! Now that he has suddenly departed, what can I rely on?” Later, when[Sun] Quanclaimed the imperial title, he told hisExcellenciesorMinisters, “Were it not forGongjin, I would not beemperor!”•••
Here, “walked alone” [獨步] is a metaphor for having no rival.↩
Kui and Shi Kuang were two masters of music in antiquity.↩
[Zhou] Yuhad two sons and one daughter. His daughter married theHeir Apparent,[Sun] Deng. His son,[Zhou] Xun, married aprincessand was conferred asCavalry Commandant. He had the demeanor of[Zhou] Yu, but died early. The younger brother of[Zhou] Xunwas[Zhou] Yin, who was first conferred asCommandant who Promotes Industry. He married an imperial clanswoman. He was given one thousand troops and garrisoned atGong’an. In the first year of theera [229], he was enfeoffed asMarquis of a Capital Township, but due to him having a misdemeanor later, he was exiled toLuling commandery. In the second year of theera [239],Zhuge JinandBu Zhijointly petitioned:
“The son of the former GeneralZhou Yu,[Zhou] Yin, had received preferential treatment14in the past, and received conferment as a general. He was unable to maintain this fortune or think about establishing meritorious deeds, but instead went so far as to indulge himself in lust and desires, incurring a misdemeanor. We ministers believe that because[Zhou] Yureceived favor and appointment in the past, he served as our heart and spine in the inside15and he acted as our claws and teeth on the outside.16He received orders to march out in expeditions, and he physically endured arrows and rocks, fully demonstrating his integrity and showing his duty to orders. He would have viewed death as a return to home. Thus, he was able to destroyCao CaoatWulinand repelCao Renat Yingdu, showing the might and virtue of the nation and causing the central plains to be shaken up. Even among the clueless Man barbarians ofJing province, there were none of them that did not submit. Though theZhouhad Fangshu17and theHanhad[Han] Xinand[Ying] Bu,18they truly could not be any more better than him. With regards to ministers who repel enemies and resists hardships, there have been no emperors and kings ever since antiquity that have not honored and respected them. In the past, theSupremeEmperorofHanmade this oath regarding fiefs and peerages: ‘Even if theYellow River[becomes tight] as a ribbon andMount Tai[becomes small] as a whetstone, your fiefdom will exist forever and pass onto your descendants.’ Declarations by cinnabar letters19and reaffirmations by pledges of alliance were kept in the imperial temples, being passed on without ever an end. The intention was to have the descendants of meritorious subjects receive inheritance generation after generation—it would not just be their sons and grandsons, but also their distant descendants—in order to repay their kindness and display their achievements. The diligence and sincerity are so extreme in such a fashion because the intention is to encourage and remind posterity that ministers who do their duties to orders can die without any regrets. What more is there to say about[Zhou] Yunot being dead for a significant time when his son,[Zhou] Yin, was reduced to but a commoner? That is quite saddening. We believe thatYour Majestysolemnly understands ancient texts and strives hard for resurrection and continuation,20and so we appeal on behalf of[Zhou] Yin. We beseech for his other crimes [to be remitted] and for his troops to be returned and his peerage to be restored. Let the rooster that missed dawn be able to cry out again. A minister who is guilty can display futureachievements.”•••
Here, “preferential treatment” [粉飾] literally means “given powder and decoration”, which is a metaphor for receiving great favor.↩
Here, “heart and spine” [心膂] is a metaphor for a trusted minister.↩
Here, “claws and teeth” [爪牙] is a metaphor for military generals.↩
Fangshu was a successful military general who expanded Zhou territory in the region aroundJing province.↩
These two military generals were instrumental in establishing theEastern Han dynasty.↩
Here, “cinnabar letters” [丹書] was a iron letter given to a subject who rendered achievements by their monarch, which was often a feudal contract between them that guaranteed the descendants of the subject would have the fiefdom. It got its name from the cinnabar powder that was used to write them.↩
These are actions Ruists thought was virtuous. TheAnalectsstates: “He has resurrected destroyed states, continued extinct lineages, and nominated secluded people. This made the people of the realm submit to him in their hearts.” [興滅國、繼絕世、擧逸民、天下之民歸心焉。]↩
[Sun] Quanreplied:
“In being a trusted aide, having past achievements, and being concord with me in many affairs,Gongjinwas all of that, and I truly have never forgotten about that. In the past,[Zhou] Yinwas young, and he did not have any initial accomplishments, so I gave him elite troops and the rank of marquis and general. It was likely because of my reminiscence overGongjinwas applied to[Zhou] Yin. However,[Zhou] Yinrelied upon them to become drunk and dissolute, acting without restraint. Despite all the warnings, never once has he repented. My relation towardsGongjinis the same as you two gentlemen had for him. I was enjoying the achievements of[Zhou] Yin; how could they have ended thus? However, in consideration of the misdeeds and malice of[Zhou] Yin, I thought it was not yet appropriate for him to return so readily. I want to have him experience bitterness so he can think about himself, but now that you two gentlemen earnestly cited the oath that theSupreme[Emperor] ofHanmade by theYellow RiverandMount Tai, I feel ashamed. Although my virtue is not equal to his, I still want to try to be similar to him. However, the situation is still as it is, hence I have not yet followed your wishes. Because he is the son ofGongjin, you two gentlemen have intervened. If he is able to change, what is there to worry about?”
[Zhuge] Jinand[Bu] Zhifrequently petitioned, andZhu RanandQuan Congalso both pleaded, so[Sun] Quanagreed to them. But it happened[Zhou] Yindied of illness.
The son of the older brother of[Zhou] Yu,[Zhou] Jun, becameDeputy Generalalso because of the prime achievements of[Zhou] Yu, and he led one thousand officials and soldiers. When[Zhou] Jundied,Quan Congpetitioned for the son of[Zhou] Jun,[Zhou] Hu, to become a general.[Sun] Quanreplied, “When I fled fromCao Caoand expanded intoJing provincenot long ago, this was all due toGongjin. Never have I forgotten that. When I first heard of the death of[Zhou] Jun, I had wanted to appoint[Zhou] Hu, but then I heard that[Zhou] Huwas of an insidious nature. If I had appointed him, he would use the opportunity to cause trouble, so I ceased. I missGongjina lot—how could I ever stop?”
translations.page_title: Zhou Yu (Gongjin) 周瑜 (公瑾) Sanguozhi Biography by ZL181 - Records of the Three Kingdoms (三國志) by Chen Shou – Kongming’s Archives

translations.source: Records of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]Scroll 54 (Wu 9)
translations.translation:
Zhou Yu (Gongjin)
周瑜 (公瑾)
Lived AD 175–210
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
Scroll 54 (Wu 9)
byBattleroyale
in2002
AbstractEntity::setUpdateForRefresh: 'update_for_refresh' value 'NULL' changed to 'true' via 'loadFlags::$this->update_for_refresh'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'published_at' changed to '1009886400' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'rendered_at' changed to '1744913682' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'updated_at' changed to '1744913643' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
AbstractEntity::updateTimestamp: Timestamp 'created_at' changed to '1744913643' from 'NULL' via 'AbstractEntity::loadEntityCache::data[db][*]'
Zhou Yu styled Gongjin, hailed from Shu (name of a place) in Lu Jiang prefecture. Two of Zhou Yu’s ancestors, Zhou Jing and Zhou Zhong held the position of Tai Wei in the Han dynasty and Zhou Yu’s father was once the magistrate of Luo Yang. Zhou Yu grew up to be a handsome lad.
In the beginning when Sun Jian was raising an army to attack Dong Zhuo, he moved his family to the Shu prefecture (where Zhou Yu resided). Zhou Yu and Sun Ce were of the same age and they were on very amicable terms. Zhou Yu invited Sun Ce to stay in his mansion and paid his respects to Sun Ce’s mother. Zhou Yu’s uncle, Zhou Shang, was the Grand Administrator of Dan Yang. Soon after, when Zhou Yu was visiting his uncle, Sun Ce was coincidentally leading his armies eastward crossing the Yang Tze river and reached Li Yang. Sun Ce sent a letter to Zhou Yu who on receiving it led some troops to meet Sun Ce. Sun Ce was overjoyed to have Zhou Yu joining his forces. Subsequently, both of them managed to capture Heng Jiang and Dang Li and they crossed the river to attack Mo Ling. There, they defeated the defenders, Ze Rong, Xue Li and proceeded south to Hu Shu and Jiang Cheng. Subsequently they entered Qu Ah, forcing Liu Yao to retreat. At this time, Sun Ce’s army numbered several tens of thousands soldiers. With this army, Sun Ce felt that he could conquer the prefectures of Wu and Hui Ji, and subdued the Shan Yue tribes. As such, he told Zhou Yu to return and defend Dan Yang.
Not long after, Yuan Shu ordered his cousin, Yuan Yin, to replace Zhou Shang as the Grand Administrator of Dan Yang. Zhou Shang, together with Zhou Yu, returned to Shou Chun. At that time, Yuan Shu contemplated on employing Zhou Yu but Zhou Yu felt that he could not achieve much by working under Yuan Shu. As such, Zhou Yu requested Yuan Shu to appoint him as the magistrate of Ju Jiao, which would give him the opportunity to return to Jiang Dong. Not knowing the true motive of Zhou Yu, Yuan Shu approved his request and gave him the appointment.
In Jian An 3rd year, Sun Ce welcomed Zhou Yu into his forces personally and conferred Zhou Yu the rank of Jian Wei Jiang Jun. In addition, he dispatched 2,000 troops and 50 horses to be placed under the command of Zhou Yu. During then, Zhou Yu was 24 years old and people in the Wu prefecture would refer to him as Zhou Lang (or “Young Gentleman Zhou”) Zhou Yu’s charitable and trustworthy nature was well known in the Lu Jiang region and he was ordered to defend Niu Zhu. Subsequently, he was given the appointment of the magistrate of Chun Gu.
Soon, Sun Ce decided to attack Jing Zhou and Zhou Yu was given the appointment of Zhong Fu Jun and the Grand Administrator of Jiang Xia. They launched an attack and captured the city of Wan. During then, Sun Ce and Zhou Yu married the daughters of Qiao Gong who were great beauties (their names were not mentioned). Following which, they reinitiated the attack at Xun Yang and defeated Liu Xun. Thereafter, they attacked Jiang Xia prefecture and diverted their armies back to subjugate Yu Zhang and Lu Ling. Zhou Yu was then tasked to defend Ba Qiu.
In Jian An 5th year, Sun Ce passed away and Sun Quan took over his place as the overall commander. Zhou Yu returned for the funeral. Back at the Wu prefecture, Zhang Zhao (who was holding the appointment of Chang Shi) and Zhou Yu were made jointly in-charge of the State’s affairs.
In Jian An 11th year, Zhou Yu, Sun Yu and some others attacked Ma Tun and Bao Tun. They beheaded their chieftains and took approximately 10,000 people in captivity. Soon after the Grand Administrator of Jiang Xia, Huang Zhu, dispatched Deng Long with several thousands soldiers to attack Chai Sang. Zhou Yu retaliated and captured Deng Long alive.
In the spring of Jian An 13th year, Sun Quan attacked Jiang Xia and appointed Zhou Yu as the commander of the front army. During this year, Cao Cao’s army invaded Jing Zhou and LiuZong, together with his officials and soldiers, surrendered to Cao Cao. As such, Cao Cao’s army was boosted by several tens of thousands infantry soldiers and the naval forces of Jing Zhou. This event frightened many of the generals in Jiang Dong and Sun Quan gathered them to seek their opinions. The majority of the generals present were in favor of surrendering. Some of their main reasons were as follows:
Some were fearful of Cao Cao’s infamous reputation from his previous exploits.Some thought that since Cao Cao had captured Jing Zhou, the kingdom of Wu could no longer rely on its geographical advantage in the form of the river Yang Tze.Cao Cao’s recent acquiring of Liu Biao’s naval fleet (with about 1,000 vessels) would substantiate his naval forces to a large extent. With that, it would be possible for Cao Cao to launch a concurrent attack from the land and via the river at Jiang Dong.The unfavorable circumstances noted in point and (iii), were made worse by the fact that Cao Cao’s army outnumbered Wu’s army.
However, Zhou Yu disagreed with those points of view. He argued that Cao Cao’s southern expedition had several weaknesses. Firstly, Zhou Yu noted that Cao Cao was actually a traitor of Han even though he was holding the appointment of the Prime Minister of Han. Cao Cao’s northern provinces were not completely stable and he had to worry about Ma Chao and Han Sui at his rear (in Guan Xi). Secondly, Cao Cao’s northern army was not familiar with naval combat unlike the southerners. Thirdly, the season at that moment was winter and the army of Cao Cao would likely be tired due to the long matches from the north. Finally, if they were not accustomed to the climate in the south, it was likely that they would be plagued with illnesses. As such, Zhou Yu felt that the situation during then provided the best opportunity to capture Cao Cao alive and he requested Sun Quan for 30,000 elite soldiers to be stationed at Xia Kou. Zhou Yu further guaranteed Sun Quan that he would be able to defeat Cao Cao. Hearing that, Sun Quan’s resolve to resist Cao Cao was strengthened.
At that moment, Liu Bei was defeated by Cao Cao and he met Lu Su at Dang Yang. In the meeting, both devised plans for alliance against Cao Cao. Subsequently, Liu Bei stationed himself at Xia Kou. In addition, Liu Bei sent Zhuge Liang as an envoy to Sun Quan. Following that, Sun Quan dispatched Zhou Yu, Cheng Pu and other Wu generals to meet Liu Bei in order to consolidate their forces against Cao Cao’s army. The army of the Sun-Liu alliance engaged Cao Cao’s army at Chi Bi. During then, Cao Cao’s army was already having problems with many soldiers falling sick. As such, during the initial engagement, Cao Cao’s army was promptly defeated and retreated back to the northern shore of the river Yang Tze.
Seeing that Cao Cao’s army was numerous, Huang Gai felt that it would be difficult to resist them for long. However, he observed that Cao Cao’s ships were linked together and he suggested to Zhou Yu that it was actually possible to repel the enemies if they launched a fire attack at them. The suggestion was accepted and preparations were made for the fireboats (boats stuffed with inflammable materials). Subsequently, a letter was sent to Cao Cao falsely claiming that Huang Gai intended to surrender. The ruse was successful and the soldiers of Wei were anticipating the arrival of Huang Gai. At that moment, Huang Gai arrived and set his fireboats on fire. Aided by the strong winds and the fact that Cao Cao’s ships were chained together, the fire quickly spread throughout Cao Cao’s navy and even to the naval camps on the shore. Within moments, the raging inferno resulted in heavy casualties in Cao Cao’s army. Cao Cao was forced to retreat and defend Nan prefecture against the victorious allied armies. Meanwhile, Liu Bei and Zhou Yu led their troops in pursuit. Nevertheless, Cao Cao himself had returned to the north after leaving Cao Ren to guard the city of Jiang Ling.
Soon after, Zhou Yu and Cheng Pu launched an attack at Nan prefecture, which was defended by Cao Ren. The attacking troops and the defenders camped on the opposite banks of the great river. Before the engagement, Zhou Yu dispatched Gan Ning to station himself at Yi Ling. Seeing that, Cao Ren divided his forces and sent troops to attack Gan Ning. Faced with Cao Ren’s forces, Lu sent a letter to Zhou Yu requesting for help. On the advice of Lü Meng, Zhou Yu left Ling Tong to guard the rear and proceeded with Lü Meng to save Gan Ning. Having relieved Gan Ning, Zhou Yu led his troops across the river and prepared for a decisive battle with Cao Ren at a specified date. On the day of battle, Zhou Yu personally led his troops into the foray but was hit by an arrow on the right side of his chest, forcing him to retreat. Cao Ren got news of Zhou Yu’s injury and decided to take the opportunity to attack Zhou Yu’s army. However, despite his injury, Zhou Yu inspected his troops and gave them words of encouragement. As a result, the morale of Zhou Yu’s army was greatly boosted and they managed to defeat Cao Ren forcing him to retreat.
Subsequently, Sun Quan appointed Zhou Yu as Pian Jiang Jun and the Grand Administrator of Nan prefecture. Zhou Yu was also ordered to station his troops at Jiang Ling for defensive purposes. Liu Bei, in the capacity of Zuo Jiang Jun (Left General) and Governor of Jing Zhou, set up his office at Gong An. When Liu Bei visited Sun Quan at Jing Kou, Zhou Yu suggested to Sun Quan to be wary of Liu Bei due to his potential and the support he got from Guan Yu and Zhang Fei. In addition, Zhou Yu wanted to have Liu Bei separated from his brothers and be positioned at Wu prefecture, giving him a life of luxury. In that way, Zhou Yu felt that he would be able to lead Zhang Fei and Guan Yu into battle thus assuring victories for future campaigns. However, Sun Quan felt that there was a need to attract more talents to his forces given the threat presented by Cao Cao in the north and harming Liu Bei would not go in line with that. Moreover, Sun Quan was not sure that Liu Bei could be controlled that easily. As a result, Zhou Yu’s advice was not heeded.
During that time, Liu Zhang was the Governor of Yi Zhou (the Xi Chuan region) and he was facing the threat of Zhang Lu. Zhou Yu visited Sun Quan at Jing Kou and presented his plan to conquer Cao Cao’s northern territories. Firstly, Zhou Yu wanted to lead an army, together with Sun Yu to capture the Shu territories. If successful, Zhou Yu planned to combine forces with Zhang Lu and positioned Sun Yu at Shu territory to link up with Ma Chao. Zhou Yu would then return to Jing Zhou and together with Sun Quan, attack Cao Cao from the direction of Xiang Yang. Sun Quan accepted his proposal and Zhou Yu returned to JiangLing to make preparations. However, Zhou Yu died of sickness on his way to Ba Qiu. He was only 36 years old then.
